sentences.text: Yeah.
About a year, maybe less.
Then I got the job in Otwock.
It's a resort suburb, not a suburb, it's in the Warsaw area about miles from the Warsaw area.
Got paid-- I got paid 100 zloty.
That's $20 a month. 100 zloty was money for me and meals, and laundry, and a room.
And I had my violin.
I had my musical library.
And I made myself a couple of nice suits, you know, custom made in Warsaw because it wasn't in Poland where you go buy a suit.
You have to buy material.
And  you have to go to a tailor until the war broke out.
Until-- until Germany attacked Poland.
He had a trucking company-- trucking company means businessmen come-- Lvov, in Lvov.
That's a 400,000-- it is a big center.
He had a-- you-- you are a businessman.
You buy your merchandise.
Or you buy anything supplies in Lvov.
Then you go to my father's office.
And he says, would you please take care of it?
That's it.
My father is employee-- he had employees.
They would load the trucks and-- and by truck, deliver it to Radziwilow, to Dubno, to surrounding towns.
He was known because he was-- and his cousin was with him in business together.
To a certain degree.
To a certain degree.
My mother-- we had a kosher home.
My mother was more religious than my father, I must say.
We had [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
So I made the choice how about [INAUDIBLE]?
She says go on, you-- you order your wife when you get married what you want.
And she [INAUDIBLE] every day-- every Friday.
And my father was traveling.
When he was home, we went to a-- to a synagogue.
There was only Orthodox synagogues.
There was no such thing as conservative or reform synagogues.
You know.
It was around the corner.
But he used to smoke, but not on Saturday.
And we-- we celebrated all the holidays strictly with the chicken over the-- over the-- on top of the head before going Yom Kippur to the-- to the-- to the synagogue, everything, you know?
Everybody was the same.
This was in Brody?
Brody.
No, no, we had a lot of friends.
We had a lot of friends.
But the people-- most of my family lived in Radziwilow, which is very close and Krzeminiec.
K-- shall I spell it?
K-R-Z-E-M-I-N-I-E-C.
That was about-- you take a train.
There was my mother's two sisters lived-- one was Fanya.
She married a doctor, Dr.
Letrock, who was an outstanding Zionist.
And-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they had two-- two children-- and-- [?
Abrasha, ?] who immigrated to Pittsburgh eventually in 1924 and [?
Katya ?] married from Warsaw engineer.
And the other sister was practically blind, a widow.
And she had a daughter in-- who lived in Briga, Latvia, and Sam [INAUDIBLE], who was a city engineer.
So every once in a  while, we went there to visit, and then my father's sister in Krzeminiec also who got married in our home.
It was funny.
They didn't have enough dowry.
So their mother-in-law for-- the mother-in-law stopped the wedding.
They needed $500 more.
So finally, with difficulties, they [? found ?] [? $500. ?] And the mother-in-law gave permission to continue with the wedding in our home under the [INAUDIBLE] and a chuppa.
I remember.
I was in high school yet.
Jewish tragic comedies, it's called-- tragic comedies.
It happens.
You know, not always there was peace in the family.
You know.
But in general, it was a very close family.
It was  a big family.
I don't remember everybody.
But I remembered the wedding-- [?
Faiga-- ?] Fanya-- [?
Faiga. ?] And she married-- she was tall.
Her husband [INAUDIBLE] was through a broker.
She was old maid.
And she was the only woman who kept house for my grandfather until he died in Radziwillow.
And she-- she couldn't get married.
She didn't have much money.
She was not-- she [? get ?] a lot of brothers.
The brothers eventually got together and married her off.
But they brought-- brought a husband for her.
He was about three feet tall-- shorter than she or two feet shorter.
It was funny when they walked around on the street.
He was walking on the tip of the toes to hold-- be able to hold her under his arm like it was supposed to be.
But they are-- they're very wonderful family.
22nd of May-- 22nd of June, 1941.
They invaded Poland.
And yeah, it's-- in 1939 September.
That's when I was in Otwock working.
I remember it was the 7th of September.
They invaded the 1st of September.
And by the 7th of September, they were bombing Warsaw and bombing Otwock.
I was helping the chest surgeon with an operation.
And suddenly, the lights went out 11 o'clock in the morning.
And bombs started to fall because there were sanatoriums, tuberculosis mostly.
They were pine woods.
And there were was a sanatorium for children.
There was a military sanatorium.
There was many private sanatoriums and resort-- and resort houses, you know, where people came in from Warsaw after a six-day work.
Seven-- you go to rest up over the weekend from the ghetto-- from the Jewish ghetto in Warsaw, which was the Jewish and Polish-- And by Poland was-- there was a district where-- Jewish district.
They call it the Jewish ghetto.
But it doesn't-- it wasn't a ghetto in the German sense of the word.
And so we decided have to go home.
And the administrator was a young man, older than I am, but he was about 35 years old and the son of Mrs.
Shagoda, he was a medical student.
And we put-- I put on two coats on my-- on myself.
I took all my documents in a little tiny suit.
I remember I didn't have a handle.
So I put-- I bound it together with a-- with a belt.
And we were walking 24 days. 24 days.
Sometimes we caught a-- a-- a freight train.
And the freight train was a blessing.
But eventually, we ran out of the-- the-- [? had the-- ?] the-- the machinists ran out of coal.
And the train didn't go any more further.
Then we have to get out, [? please, ?] out.
And we walked 24 days.
7th of-- 7th of-- of-- the 29th of June they invaded-- no, in September. 7th of-- 7th of-- 8th of September, we left.
The 7th of September they were bombing already, not only Warsaw, but the surrounding areas like Otwock.
So the next day, we decided out-- we have to get out.
Didn't understand nothing.
Nobody knew anything.
There was no communication.
They didn't-- nobody knew about concentration camps.
Nobody knew about knew about ditches and the-- killing Jews, throwing them in the ditch.
Or they were throwing themselves in the ditch and standing on the edge of the edge of the ditch and shooting them in the back.
Nobody knew because there was-- we will survive.
As a matter of fact, it happens when-- when they came and invaded Russia in 1941-- in-- in 22nd of June, 1941.
So yeah--
I went to my home, to-- to Brody.
My parents lived in Brody.
And I was near Warsaw-- close to Warsaw.
But it was  a suburban train to Warsaw.
And like I said, I walked 24 days with other-- the other two boys.
The Russians were there already.
The Russians invaded Poland-- when-- or-- yeah.
A year before Germany invaded Poland, in 1939-- it was 1938, Ribbentrop, the minister-- the German minister of internal affairs and Stalin.
Ribbentrop went to Warsaw.
And they made a-- a pact.
We will divide Poland, the Curzon line.
What is west of Curzon line will be German.
What is east of Curzon line-- I'm talking about dividing Poland-- it will be Russia.
My father was home.
My mother was home.
And my-- and my mother was happy to see me because I didn't have no-- I didn't-- I didn't have no communication-- a month she didn't hear from me-- no telephone, no nothing.
Warsaw was bombed something terrible.
Then I saw a rest stop.
I-- I wasn't sick.
It didn't bother me. 24 days working, I-- I felt like a million dollars.
I lived on green cucumbers dug up from the-- from the orchards.
And it was a beautiful September weather.
It was excellent.
We were young boys.
I was-- I was 27 years old.
Then there's not time-- not enough-- no time to rest more.
I had to go to see something to get work.
And I passed by a street.
And there is a-- a-- a shingle, a doctor-- a doctor shingle.
I come into the doctor.
Maybe he would take me for an assistant.
I talked to the lady.
She says, where is doctor-- a Polish doctor?
Where is Dr.
Winslowski ?] or something.
Oh, he's in the army-- in Polish Army.
They drafted him.
I said, did you hear from him?
They said, no.
And the end result was he was killed.
I came in three times there.
But then she got notice-- she got notice from some friends.
I don't know-- a roundabout way.
She was a widow and started to cry.
So I said, I'll tell you.
I'm a doctor.
I'll rent the office from you.
And 20% of what I  make, I'll pay your rent-- not rent, just 20% from the profit.
She said, sure.
There was complete office arranged with all the instruments.
There was a waiting room.
There was  a couch.
There was all things-- everything-- examining tables, syringes, everything that a doctor needs.
OK, I'll give you-- give me-- I'll give you-- she didn't ask for anything-- no-- no contract or anything.
She gave me a key to the office.
It had a separate entrance.
I took the key, locked the office, and I started-- I'm going to start office hours tomorrow.
I came home.
And I told my mother and father I'm a doctor.
I have an office.
Mazel tov, mazel tov, mazel tov!
All right,  next day, I come in there.
There is another lock on the office.
So I said, what happened?
So I say, two officers came, broke off the lock, put their own lock.
And they said it's-- we are requisitioning the office to sleep-- to live there because there was a couch, and chairs, and blankets.
One slept on the floor.
One slept on the-- on the-- on the examining couch.
What kind of officers were they?
Russian officers.
That's it.
So my career is end-- end of my career to practice medicine.
I was bitter.
So I go out to the Communist-- to the party office, which is the secretary of the party in Russia is the-- the supreme being over-- above the city manager, above everybody.
I come in to him, to the secretary-- to the Communist party office.
And I say, I want to talk to the secretary.
The secretary is like God in the-- in the-- in the district.
And he's a-- he's a nice guy.
I told my name is Dr.-- my name is Brand.
I am a doctor.
I just-- I'm a refugee.
I told him the whole story.
And I rented an office.
And I was going to open the office for patients to see.
And the two officers invaded my office.
And I have no office.
That's terrible.
So he said, yeah, this is terrible.
I agreed-- he agreed with me.
See what I can do.
[INAUDIBLE] every doctor is a specialist.
That's-- they call them specialists.
You're a specialist, and they took away your office?
Terrible.
Terrible.
Come tomorrow and see what-- what I can do you-- very friendly.
I come in tomorrow.
He says, I can do nothing.
It's the war.
It's the army.
They are a priority.
That's it.
That's the end of it.
Then I got another break.
You see, the Polish Army was retreating east, running away from the Germans.
When we were walking 24 days, the army was running away.
The civilians were all going east.
The Russians came in from the east going west.
So any-- so any person in a military uniform, Polish uniform, they took him as a prisoner, although there was no war between Russia and Poland.
And they had thousands and thousands.
The officers they segregated.
The officers were enemies of the people in Russian opinion-- in the opinion of the Communists.
They put them in concentration camps.
There's the famous camp of Katyn.
They killed 3,000 Polish officers.
They said the Germans did it.
But the Russians did it.
Beria was the KGB, the head of, you know, the KGB and signed an order them to be killed.
Beria.
Beria.
He was not Russian.
He was-- from Turkistan or from Georgia, from Georgia or something like that.
Huh?
B-E-R-I-A.
Beria.
Beria.
He was the head of the [INAUDIBLE] or KGB, you know, secret service.
[INAUDIBLE].
Anyway, they had several thousand-- 20,000, 30,000 Russian-- Polish prisoners of war-- polish soldiers-- prisoners of war.
The reason they took them-- they were building a strategic highway from Kiev to the Curzon line to the German border.
They were building a strategic highway.
And a strategic highway requires a lot of stones to be broken and built.
There was about 800 miles.
So  they needed these people to work on this highway.
So they took them like prisoners of war.
It was not-- not fair.
But that's what happened.
And they organized camps-- work camps.
And it happened so they needed doctors in these camps.
This-- like, there was a camp of 5,000 people.
And this camp was in-- in four points.
And each camp-- each district had 5,000.
Let's say the 5th District was 5,000 Polish prisoners of war, they called them.
But they had-- had five camps in this district.
I got acquainted with a-- with a Russian officer, a Jewish Russian officer.
They were coming to our house for coffee, tea.
The-- the chief engineer of this construction was a Jewish engineer, Berdachevsky.
He is from Berdichev-- Berdachevsky-- a wonderful man.
He was a, yeah, member of the party because he had a responsible job.
And also, there was a political overseer.
Every organization has a politruk.
That means a political manager.
And they worked together.
The-- the-- the professional head of the job was Berdichevsky, was engineer.
And they-- and they-- this-- this man who was the member of the party.
So he was-- he worked-- they worked together.
And they came in, got acquainted somehow.
He says, you are a doctor?
Yeah, we need a doctor.
All right.
We pay five times as much because that's for the-- and-- Ministry of Internal Affairs.
But the Department of Building Strategic Highways had nothing to do with secret service.
So they gave me a job.
And I was the head of the-- head doctor for those 5,000-- the prisoners of war.
In five points, I got a pair of horses, and a-- and a-- and a car, and special restaurant to eat, and a secretary, and I had a big shot, and paid me.
And I had-- and there was such a supply house-- supply store where they gave me certain materials to make a pair of pants, and buy groceries, and buy toiletries.
And so forth.

But my-- my secretary was not allowed to go where I go, because she was only a secretary-- a-- a smaller portion of material for a skirt, a different restaurant-- that's the-- the Communist, you know, equality.
And that's-- and then-- and then I got the job for my father there-- head of the warehouse of instrument-- tools-- tools.
Because-- and they-- the officers-- the prisoners were treated very well.
Once a week, they went to bath-- to the-- to take baths, and delousing machine-- delousing such containers, and food.
And no-- no physical abuse.
You know.
But they had to go to work every week-- every day in the morning, 6 o'clock, under guard.
There's guards.
They had to go to the-- to the-- road and work for eight hours.
But they had-- they got a cold lunch.
And they got a-- a hot dinner.
And when you needed laundry or something-- when your shirt got torn to be thrown away, they gave you a shirt.
They gave you underwear until the-- till 1941 when the Germans invaded Russia.
They broke their agreement.
They-- they-- they manufactured-- Germans-- Gestapo-- Hitler manufactured an incident in Gdansk.
That was the port on the Baltic Sea-- a Polish port in the Baltic Sea.
And there was a little island, tiny, little island.
Evidently, it was an island of-- not for-- for military purposes.
It was a small, little island.
So the Germans manufactured an incident that this island they-- they kidnapped some Germans.
The Polish kidnapped some Germans and put them on the island.
And they mistreated them and killed one.
That's all.
And-- and Hitler was ready.
He gave an order for invasion of Poland and Russia a year before they invaded-- 1938.
As soon as they signed the agreement with-- Ribbentrop signed with Stalin, he had an order-- a secret order from Stalin.
Get ready for invasion of Poland a year from now.
And then he decided to invade Russia like he decided to invade Belgium, and Holland, and France, and-- and Greece, and Bulgaria, and Yugoslavia, and Italy.
Yeah, they walked into Brody.
Second-- next day, there were big announcements in German and in Polish that the Jews are not supposed-- not allowed to walk on sidewalks.
The Jews have to wear a Magen David on the left arm.
The Jews are not allowed to collect any rations or go into private stores to buy food.
And then the started-- then the Jews have to move to a future ghetto.
That-- and then-- in the meantime, and the war was going on continuously.
In fact, the first order of the Gestapo-- I remember they walked into the town.
The first-- the first group of Polish, you know, the-- the first group of motorcyclists came in.
I was on the street.
And the same afternoon, the communications were not-- they were bombing still.
But then there were no more because the Gestapo took  over the town.
We-- yeah, we expected it, because when I-- where I came from, there were the Germans already was a long time there.
By the time I came home from the time I left Warsaw, when they were bombing Warsaw to smithereens, you know.
And they were close to Warsaw.
It was 24 days-- 22 days, because by the time-- or maybe less, because when I came home, the Russians were there already.
You know.
In the end, the 20-- then they-- in other words, they walked through Poland.
Germans walked through Poland 20 days.
In 20 days.
Because they invaded Russia the 22nd of June.
They invaded Poland the 1st  of September, 1939.
And then the 22nd of June, 1941, they invaded Russia.
They were done with-- with Poland.
And they were already-- they were already in Czechoslovakia.
Not Czechoslovakia, they invaded in 1930-- '38.
[INAUDIBLE] 1937 in Austria, they took over Austria first then Sudeten Deutschland, which was part of Czechoslovakia.
And next was Poland.
Scared to death-- scared to death.
The next day-- three-- or two days later, there were announced of anti-Jewish laws.
Yes.
Yeah, of course, the word travels.
You know, people were running away from Lodz, from different towns, you know, from-- from the west part of Poland.
No-- I mean, it was common knowledge.
You know.
But nobody taught that somehow, you know, my parents-- I could take my parents to Siberia, because I was a government worker.
And they offered civilian trains for civilian-- especially for people who worked for them.
I worked for the KGB-- KGB was a big-- the Ministry of Internal of Affairs, the Department of Strategic Highways.
But I was a government worker.
And my chief said, you can-- you can take your parents.
Of course they was-- they have to be three months on the road.
You know.
But my mother didn't want to leave the furniture.
We will survive.
We will survive.
Nobody-- there was no communication.
We didn't know anybody about killing 100, 200, 300 people at once.
We didn't know about concentration camps.
We know that they didn't like Jews.
There were anti-Jewish laws.
But there was deliberate, organized destruction of the Jewish nation.
There was nothing like that even when Nebuchadnezzar invaded Israel.
He sent them to Babylon.
No.
The same.
They were not Jewish friends.
They were not Jewish friends.
The Germans got a lot of help from the Ukrainians because there was, right away, they organized a Ukrainian police.
They promise them that there will be an Ukrainian country.
And there will be Ukrainian head, which will be appointed by the Germans.
There will be Ukrainian police.
When the Germans started to mistreat Jews or when they had to-- when they decided-- we need-- they-- they came to the Judenrat.
They said, we need 400 Jews tomorrow so-- to kill them.
The Ukrainian police helped.
